.engine/operations/boot-session-start.md.why: boot-session-start is the heaviest member of the orientation family — the SessionStart pack a cold session is meant to read in full, every boot. Its length is load-bearing operator-facing content: the anti-habituation collapse rules and five distinct repair-offer contracts in Notes — un-stranding, catch-up, off-main return, stranded-pull-request reconcile, and branch-protection re-enable — each a real behavior carrying its own tool name, loss semantics, and issue/decision reference, none redundant with another. At 121 body lines it is the second-largest operation runbook after build-orchestration; every other is 106 lines or fewer. This knowingly revisits the call at issue #364 (commit 0eb5262), which trimmed rather than raised — the right call then, because that overage was genuine repetition, which #364 removed (landing the file at 115). The return over budget came from #392 adding the fifth repair contract: real capability growth, not the repetition #364 cut, so raising beats trimming here. The ceiling of 150 is the operator's deliberate choice, weighed at the plan gate against a tighter ceiling and chosen for room to grow as the boot-surfaced repair set grows — well short of build-orchestration's 250. It is still a ceiling, not a removal: the soft nudge fires again above 150, and this rule's hard tier still fails a malformed or reasonless override, so a dead grant cannot silently accumulate. The raise lives in this guarded rule, so it needs the operator's deliberate sign-off — the recorded-reason pattern issue #273 asked for, and the resolution of issue #460 (a recorded ceiling rather than a runbook silently sitting over budget, or its load-bearing prose shaved to fit).

.engine/operations/build-orchestration.m.why: build-orchestration is the convergence runbook — where every other system's build work is actually carried out (plan, review, integrate, submit, routine, reconcile). The 120-line operations nudge starves the one runbook that legitimately spans all of those, so it carries a raised ceiling: deliberate room to grow as the build process it documents grows, not a tight fit to today's length. The ceiling moved 200 to 215 when the runbook gained the consumed-review-lenses record plus the dry-run disclosure line, and 215 to 250 when issue #414 completed the plan gate against the locked design — adding the fast-path floor and its guardrail/schema exclusion, the proportionate progress-view checklist tier, the two-input coverage proposal (what review is installed and what sits next to the work; the operator-preference input was later removed to keep the depth recommendation risk-only), the advise-re-audit rider, the Plan-time decomposability recommendation, and the fifth step-rendering rule (a step must be able to fail). All of it is load-bearing build-process content that would otherwise force trimming a standing operator-disclosure paragraph to fit. The raise lives in this guarded rule, so changing it needs the operator's deliberate sign-off — which is what issue #273 asked for: a recorded reason, rather than a runbook silently sitting over budget.

message: An operation (a runbook under .engine/operations/) must contain, as level-2 headings in this order, Purpose, then Steps, then Done when, and it may also contain a Notes section, and no others. These sections are what make a runbook followable — what it is for, the ordered steps, and how to know it is done — so an operation that omits a required one is structurally incomplete. This check catches a broken SHAPE only: that the required sections are present, correctly ordered, and that no stray section appears. It cannot judge whether a section's content is substantive — an empty or hand-wavy section passes here and is caught instead by the operator reviewing the committed change, and whether the runbook actually tells a cold operator how to perform the procedure is the audits layer's judgment, not this check's. Length over an operation's line budget is only a soft nudge to trim, never a block; the budget is 120 by default, and a specific operation may carry a higher recorded budget in length_budget_overrides. A malformed or stale override entry — one missing its budget or recorded reason, or naming no operation — is the rule's hard tier, so a dead budget grant cannot accumulate. Add or reorder the flagged sections to match the operation template (.engine/templates/operation.md) before merging.
